sources.issuing_organisation: National Institute for Health and Care Excellence
sources.exact_document_title: Irritable bowel syndrome in adults: diagnosis and management
sources.version: NICE CG61; last updated 2017-04-04; management update reviewed 2025-04-30
sources.population: Adults with suspected or confirmed irritable bowel syndrome.
sources.clinical_setting: Primary-care assessment, investigation, management, and follow-up.
sources.applicability_note: Exact for selected adult IBS symptom-profile, investigation, lifestyle-discussion, and follow-up fields; the legacy workflow does not enforce adult age or the required symptom duration and diagnostic criteria.

exact_sections.heading: Recommendations 1.1.1.1 and 1.1.1.4 — IBS symptom profile
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports at least 6 months of abdominal pain or discomfort, bloating, bowel-habit change, relation to defaecation or stool frequency or form, straining, urgency, incomplete evacuation, food association, mucus, and selected extra-intestinal symptoms.

exact_sections.heading: Recommendation 1.1.1.2 — red-flag assessment
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Requires clinical assessment and examination for suspected-cancer features and inflammatory markers for inflammatory bowel disease, with referral when present; specific cancer-feature fields require their own exact source.

exact_sections.heading: Recommendations 1.1.2.1–1.1.2.2 — diagnostic tests
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports full blood count, ESR or plasma viscosity, CRP, and coeliac antibodies in people meeting IBS criteria and identifies tests not required solely to confirm IBS.

exact_sections.heading: Recommendations 1.2.1.1–1.2.1.8 — dietary and lifestyle advice
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports clinician-documented lifestyle, activity, diet, symptom-trigger, and specialist dietary discussion; specific advice is not inserted automatically.

exact_sections.heading: Recommendation 1.2.5.1 — follow-up
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports agreed symptom-response-based follow-up, annual review, and investigation or referral when new red-flag symptoms emerge.
